1:
"Now Jericho was straitly shut up because of the children of Israel: none went out, and none came in." The witnesses note Jericho shut up tight against Israel, none going out or in. The witnesses note that Gill notes the king and inhabitants shut it up within, the Targum says with iron doors and bars of brass, and it was blocked up without by Israel. The witnesses note that none went out and none came in. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the straitly shut up — barred within; the because of Israel — sealed against them; the none out, none in — a fortified obstacle; the the victory God's — not by might, but by his Spirit (Zech. 4:6).

The witnesses agree the verse sets the seeming impossibility of the task before God's power: Jericho was "straitly shut up," barred within and besieged without, a strong city sealed against Israel; the human hopelessness of taking so fortified a place by their means makes the coming victory plainly God's; it teaches that God often sets his people before an impossible obstacle, that the deliverance may be owned as his alone, the shut city about to fall not by their might but by his power, "not by might, nor by power, but by my spirit, saith the LORD" (Zech. 4:6).

So the witnesses make the verse Jericho shut up: The witnesses note the straitly shut up: barred within. The witnesses note the because of Israel: sealed against them. The witnesses note the none out, none in: a fortified obstacle. The witnesses note the the victory God's: not by might, but by his Spirit (Zech. 4:6). The expositors observe the straitly shut up: barred within. The expositors observe the because of Israel: sealed against them. The expositors observe the none out, none in: a fortified obstacle. The expositors observe the the victory God's: not by might, but by his Spirit (Zech. 4:6). The believer is taught to mark the straitly shut up, the because of Israel, the none out, none in, the the victory God's, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that Jericho was straitly shut up against Israel — the seeming impossibility setting off God's power, the city to fall not by their might but by his, not by might but by his Spirit (Zech. 4:6).
2:
"And the LORD said unto Joshua, See, I have given into thine hand Jericho, and the king thereof, and the mighty men of valour." The witnesses note the LORD — the Captain of the host — assuring Joshua the city is already given into his hand. The witnesses note that Gill notes the same divine Person called the Captain of the Lord's host now gives Joshua these orders and assurances. The witnesses note that the LORD said, I have given Jericho into thine hand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD said — the Captain himself; the I have given — the victory already granted; the the king and mighty men — all reckoned Joshua's; the faith on God's word — the victory through Christ (1 Cor. 15:57).

The witnesses agree the conquest began with the promise spoken as already accomplished: "I have given into thine hand Jericho" — the victory granted before the first step, the city, king, and mighty men reckoned Joshua's by God's word; Gill notes the speaker is the Captain of the LORD's host, the divine Person of the previous chapter; the past tense of the promise teaches that faith goes to its task on the ground of God's sure word, the victory secured beforehand; it bids the believer take up the battle counting it won, "thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ" (1 Cor. 15:57).

So the witnesses make the verse I have given Jericho: The witnesses note the the LORD said: the Captain himself. The witnesses note the I have given: the victory already granted. The witnesses note the the king and mighty men: all reckoned Joshua's. The witnesses note the faith on God's word: the victory through Christ (1 Cor. 15:57). The expositors observe the the LORD said: the Captain himself. The expositors observe the I have given: the victory already granted. The expositors observe the the king and mighty men: all reckoned Joshua's. The expositors observe the faith on God's word: the victory through Christ (1 Cor. 15:57). The believer is taught to mark the the LORD said, the I have given, the the king and mighty men, the faith on God's word, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that the LORD assured Joshua the city was already given into his hand — the victory secured by God's word before the battle, faith going to its task counting it won, the victory through Christ (1 Cor. 15:57).
3:
"And ye shall compass the city, all ye men of war, and go round about the city once. Thus shalt thou do six days." The witnesses note the strange command — to march around the city once a day for six days. The witnesses note that Gill notes all able for war, above twenty, were to compass the city, not in a siege but by a procession around it. The witnesses note that this was to be done six days. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the compass the city — a daily march; the all ye men of war — the solemn procession; the once... six days — no siege or assault; the faith obeys the strange — the foolishness of God wiser than men (1 Cor. 1:25).

The witnesses agree the means appointed were strange and to human reason useless — a daily march around the walls: "ye shall compass the city... once... six days," no engines, no assault, but a solemn procession; the very oddness of the command was a test of obedience and faith, that the victory might be plainly God's and not man's contriving; it teaches that God's ways of working are often unlike man's, and that faith obeys even where the means seem foolish, "the foolishness of God is wiser than men" (1 Cor. 1:25), the marching feet to do what no battering-ram could.

So the witnesses make the verse compass the city: The witnesses note the compass the city: a daily march. The witnesses note the all ye men of war: the solemn procession. The witnesses note the once... six days: no siege or assault. The witnesses note the faith obeys the strange: the foolishness of God wiser than men (1 Cor. 1:25). The expositors observe the compass the city: a daily march. The expositors observe the all ye men of war: the solemn procession. The expositors observe the once... six days: no siege or assault. The expositors observe the faith obeys the strange: the foolishness of God wiser than men (1 Cor. 1:25). The believer is taught to mark the compass the city, the all ye men of war, the once... six days, the faith obeys the strange, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that they were to march around the city once a day for six days — the strange means a test of faith, the victory to be plainly God's, faith obeying where the means seem foolish (1 Cor. 1:25).
4:
"And seven priests shall bear before the ark seven trumpets of rams' horns: and the seventh day ye shall compass the city seven times, and the priests shall blow with the trumpets." The witnesses note the ark borne in the procession, seven priests with seven trumpets, the seventh day seven times. The witnesses note that Gill, with Ben Gersom, notes the ark, carried by priests, was to direct Israel to keep all written in the law contained in it. The witnesses note that the seventh day they were to compass seven times. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seven priests — before the ark; the seven trumpets — of rams' horns; the the seventh day seven times — the perfect number; the God's presence the power — let God arise, his enemies scattered (Ps. 68:1).

The witnesses agree the ark and the trumpets set God's presence and word at the centre of the conquest: "seven priests... before the ark... seven trumpets," and on the seventh day "seven times"; the ark, holding the law, marked that God himself went before his people, and the repeated sevens the perfection and completeness of the divine working; the procession was no act of war but of worship and faith, God's presence the power that would throw down the walls; it teaches that the victories of God's people are won by his presence going before them, the ark in the midst, "let God arise, let his enemies be scattered" (Ps. 68:1).

So the witnesses make the verse the ark and trumpets: The witnesses note the seven priests: before the ark. The witnesses note the seven trumpets: of rams' horns. The witnesses note the the seventh day seven times: the perfect number. The witnesses note the God's presence the power: let God arise, his enemies scattered (Ps. 68:1). The expositors observe the seven priests: before the ark. The expositors observe the seven trumpets: of rams' horns. The expositors observe the the seventh day seven times: the perfect number. The expositors observe the God's presence the power: let God arise, his enemies scattered (Ps. 68:1). The believer is taught to mark the seven priests, the seven trumpets, the the seventh day seven times, the God's presence the power, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that the ark and seven trumpets led the procession, the seventh day seven times — God's presence and word at the centre, the victory won by his presence going before, let God arise and his enemies be scattered (Ps. 68:1).
5:
"And it shall come to pass, that when they make a long blast with the ram's horn... all the people shall shout with a great shout; and the wall of the city shall fall down flat..." The witnesses note the promise — at the long blast and the people's shout, the wall shall fall down flat. The witnesses note that Gill notes the long blast, drawn out, was made only on the seventh day, different from the short blasts of the other days. The witnesses note that the wall should fall down flat and the people go up. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a long blast — on the seventh day; the all shall shout — the shout of faith; the the wall fall flat — not by force; the faith's power — by faith the walls fell (Heb. 11:30).

The witnesses agree the verse holds the great promise of the chapter — the wall to fall not by force but at the shout of faith: "all the people shall shout... and the wall... shall fall down flat"; no engine or hand would topple it, but the trumpet-blast and the believing shout, at God's word; the falling of the walls is the very example the apostle gives of faith's power, "by faith the walls of Jericho fell down, after they were compassed about seven days" (Heb. 11:30); it teaches that the strongholds set against God's people fall before faith and his power, the shout of faith mightier than all their walls.

So the witnesses make the verse the wall shall fall: The witnesses note the a long blast: on the seventh day. The witnesses note the all shall shout: the shout of faith. The witnesses note the the wall fall flat: not by force. The witnesses note the faith's power: by faith the walls fell (Heb. 11:30). The expositors observe the a long blast: on the seventh day. The expositors observe the all shall shout: the shout of faith. The expositors observe the the wall fall flat: not by force. The expositors observe the faith's power: by faith the walls fell (Heb. 11:30). The believer is taught to mark the a long blast, the all shall shout, the the wall fall flat, the faith's power, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that at the long blast and the people's shout the wall should fall flat — the strongholds falling not by force but before faith and God's power, by faith the walls of Jericho fell (Heb. 11:30).
6:
"And Joshua the son of Nun called the priests, and said unto them, Take up the ark of the covenant, and let seven priests bear seven trumpets of rams' horns before the ark of the LORD." The witnesses note Joshua calling the priests to take up the ark and bear the trumpets as commanded. The witnesses note that Gill notes not the Levites and Kohathites whose work it commonly was, but the priests on this occasion, as many as sufficient. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the communicating of the command. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Joshua called the priests — to carry out the command; the take up the ark — the order obeyed; the seven trumpets — borne before the ark; the exact obedience — done as the LORD commanded (Exod. 39:42).

The witnesses agree Joshua at once set about the exact carrying-out of the divine command: he "called the priests" and ordered the ark taken up and the trumpets borne, just as the LORD had appointed; the prompt and faithful obedience of the leader, putting the ark and the priests in their place, shows his reverent care to do all as God directed; it teaches that the work of God is to be done in God's way, the leader careful to obey every detail of the command, faith expressing itself in exact obedience, "according to all that the LORD commanded... so did they" (cf. Exod. 39:42).

So the witnesses make the verse take up the ark: The witnesses note the Joshua called the priests: to carry out the command. The witnesses note the take up the ark: the order obeyed. The witnesses note the seven trumpets: borne before the ark. The witnesses note the exact obedience: done as the LORD commanded (Exod. 39:42). The expositors observe the Joshua called the priests: to carry out the command. The expositors observe the take up the ark: the order obeyed. The expositors observe the seven trumpets: borne before the ark. The expositors observe the exact obedience: done as the LORD commanded (Exod. 39:42). The believer is taught to mark the Joshua called the priests, the take up the ark, the seven trumpets, the exact obedience, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that Joshua called the priests to take up the ark and bear the trumpets — prompt and exact obedience, the leader careful to do all as God directed, faith in exact obedience (Exod. 39:42).
7:
"And he said unto the people, Pass on, and compass the city, and let him that is armed pass on before the ark of the LORD." The witnesses note Joshua ordering the people, armed and unarmed, to compass the city before the ark. The witnesses note that Gill notes the people here are the women, children, and unarmed, distinguished from the armed men. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the people passing on to compass the city. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the pass on — at Joshua's word; the compass the city — the procession formed; the the armed before the ark — all in order; the all the people — sharing in the cause (Heb. 11:30).

The witnesses agree the whole congregation, armed and unarmed, took its place in the solemn procession around the ark: the armed men before, the ark in the midst, the people following, all moving together at Joshua's word; the joining of the whole people in the act shows that the conquest was the work of the whole congregation in faith, not of warriors only; it teaches that all God's people have their part in his cause, gathered about his presence, the procession of faith encircling the doomed city, the whole camp under the leading of the ark, "compassed... about" in believing obedience (cf. Heb. 11:30).

So the witnesses make the verse compass the city: The witnesses note the pass on: at Joshua's word. The witnesses note the compass the city: the procession formed. The witnesses note the the armed before the ark: all in order. The witnesses note the all the people: sharing in the cause (Heb. 11:30). The expositors observe the pass on: at Joshua's word. The expositors observe the compass the city: the procession formed. The expositors observe the the armed before the ark: all in order. The expositors observe the all the people: sharing in the cause (Heb. 11:30). The believer is taught to mark the pass on, the compass the city, the the armed before the ark, the all the people, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that Joshua ordered the whole people to compass the city before the ark — the conquest the work of the whole congregation in faith, all gathered about God's presence in believing obedience (Heb. 11:30).
8:
"And it came to pass, when Joshua had spoken unto the people, that the seven priests bearing the seven trumpets of rams' horns passed on before the LORD, and blew with the trumpets: and the ark of the covenant of the LORD followed them." The witnesses note the priests blowing the trumpets and the ark following, the procession begun. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua had finished the orders and directions given the people. The witnesses note that the priests blew and the ark of the covenant followed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the priests passed on — before the LORD; the blew the trumpets — the sacred signal; the the ark followed — god's presence central; the the LORD goes before — the sound of a trumpet (Ps. 47:5).

The witnesses agree the procession moved off in order, the trumpets sounding before the ark: "the seven priests... passed on before the LORD, and blew with the trumpets: and the ark... followed"; the blowing of the trumpets "before the LORD" — the ark his throne and presence — marked the whole as a sacred act done in his sight; the order of the march, with the ark central, shows God himself leading the host; it teaches that the people of God go forth to their warfare under his presence and to the sound of his appointed signal, the trumpets and the ark proclaiming that the LORD goes before, "God is gone up with a shout, the LORD with the sound of a trumpet" (Ps. 47:5).

So the witnesses make the verse the procession begun: The witnesses note the the priests passed on: before the LORD. The witnesses note the blew the trumpets: the sacred signal. The witnesses note the the ark followed: God's presence central. The witnesses note the the LORD goes before: the sound of a trumpet (Ps. 47:5). The expositors observe the the priests passed on: before the LORD. The expositors observe the blew the trumpets: the sacred signal. The expositors observe the the ark followed: God's presence central. The expositors observe the the LORD goes before: the sound of a trumpet (Ps. 47:5). The believer is taught to mark the the priests passed on, the blew the trumpets, the the ark followed, the the LORD goes before, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that the priests blew the trumpets and the ark followed — the procession a sacred act done before the LORD, God himself leading the host, gone up with the sound of a trumpet (Ps. 47:5).
9:
"And the armed men went before the priests that blew with the trumpets, and the rereward came after the ark, the priests going on, and blowing with the trumpets." The witnesses note the order of the march — the armed men before, the rereward behind the ark, the trumpets sounding throughout. The witnesses note that Gill notes the armed men, some interpret of Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh, others of all the armed men in the camp. The witnesses note that the rereward came after the ark, the trumpets blowing continually. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the armed before — the vanguard; the the rereward after — the ark guarded behind; the the trumpets blowing — sounding throughout; the God in the midst — the LORD encampeth round about them (Ps. 34:7).

The witnesses agree the careful ordering of the procession set the ark and the trumpets at the heart of the host, guarded before and behind: "the armed men... before the priests... and the rereward... after the ark"; the placing of the ark in the midst, with the people fore and aft and the trumpets ever sounding, made the whole march a moving testimony to God's presence among them; it teaches that God dwells in the midst of his people and is their defence on every side, encompassed by them as they by him, "the angel of the LORD encampeth round about them that fear him" (Ps. 34:7), the ark central and the trumpets unceasing.

So the witnesses make the verse the order of march: The witnesses note the the armed before: the vanguard. The witnesses note the the rereward after: the ark guarded behind. The witnesses note the the trumpets blowing: sounding throughout. The witnesses note the God in the midst: the LORD encampeth round about them (Ps. 34:7). The expositors observe the the armed before: the vanguard. The expositors observe the the rereward after: the ark guarded behind. The expositors observe the the trumpets blowing: sounding throughout. The expositors observe the God in the midst: the LORD encampeth round about them (Ps. 34:7). The believer is taught to mark the the armed before, the the rereward after, the the trumpets blowing, the God in the midst, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that the armed men went before and the rereward after the ark, the trumpets ever sounding — God's presence at the heart of the host, his people encompassing the ark as he defends them round about (Ps. 34:7).
10:
"And Joshua had commanded the people, saying, Ye shall not shout, nor make any noise with your voice... until the day I bid you shout; then shall ye shout." The witnesses note the charge to keep silence until the appointed moment to shout. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was given when he ordered them to compass the city. The witnesses note that Wesley notes shouting before the time would be ineffectual, discouraging themselves and giving the enemy matter of insult. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ye shall not shout — keep silence; the nor make any noise — till the appointed time; the until I bid you — waiting God's hour; the patient obedience — he that believeth shall not make haste (Isa. 28:16).

The witnesses agree the strict charge to silence until the bidden moment was a discipline of patient, exact obedience: "ye shall not shout... until the day I bid you"; Wesley notes that a premature shout would be vain and give the enemy occasion to mock; the people must wait God's time, doing all in his order and not before; it teaches the wisdom and necessity of patience in God's service, that we wait his appointed hour and act only at his word, "he that believeth shall not make haste" (Isa. 28:16), the silence kept till the moment of victory.

So the witnesses make the verse keep silence: The witnesses note the ye shall not shout: keep silence. The witnesses note the nor make any noise: till the appointed time. The witnesses note the until I bid you: waiting God's hour. The witnesses note the patient obedience: he that believeth shall not make haste (Isa. 28:16). The expositors observe the ye shall not shout: keep silence. The expositors observe the nor make any noise: till the appointed time. The expositors observe the until I bid you: waiting God's hour. The expositors observe the patient obedience: he that believeth shall not make haste (Isa. 28:16). The believer is taught to mark the ye shall not shout, the nor make any noise, the until I bid you, the patient obedience, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that the people were to keep silence till bidden to shout — the discipline of patient, exact obedience, waiting God's appointed hour, he that believeth shall not make haste (Isa. 28:16).
11:
"So the ark of the LORD compassed the city, going about it once: and they came into the camp, and lodged in the camp." The witnesses note the ark compassing the city once that first day, the people returning to camp. The witnesses note that Gill notes the ark, borne by the priests, was carried round the city, or, as Kimchi, Joshua caused the ark to compass it. The witnesses note that they came into the camp and lodged. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the ark compassed — the city once; the going about it once — the first day's march; the returned to camp — no wall yet fallen; the faith perseveres — need of patience to receive the promise (Heb. 10:36).

The witnesses agree the first day's march ended quietly, the ark having compassed the city once: they went round and returned to camp, no wall yet fallen, no visible result; the unspectacular close of the first day was a trial of faith, the people obeying without seeing any effect; it teaches that faith continues in God's appointed way though no fruit yet appears, the believer persevering in obedience while the promise tarries, "ye have need of patience, that... ye might receive the promise" (Heb. 10:36), the ark compassing the city and the people waiting on.

So the witnesses make the verse compassed once: The witnesses note the the ark compassed: the city once. The witnesses note the going about it once: the first day's march. The witnesses note the returned to camp: no wall yet fallen. The witnesses note the faith perseveres: need of patience to receive the promise (Heb. 10:36). The expositors observe the the ark compassed: the city once. The expositors observe the going about it once: the first day's march. The expositors observe the returned to camp: no wall yet fallen. The expositors observe the faith perseveres: need of patience to receive the promise (Heb. 10:36). The believer is taught to mark the the ark compassed, the going about it once, the returned to camp, the faith perseveres, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that the ark compassed the city once and the people returned — the first day ending without visible result, faith continuing in God's way though no fruit yet appeared, needing patience to receive the promise (Heb. 10:36).
12:
"And Joshua rose early in the morning, and the priests took up the ark of the LORD." The witnesses note Joshua rising early on the second day, diligent to do God's work. The witnesses note that Gill notes he rose early to take care of, direct, and prepare everything for another procession; so active and diligent was he to do the will and work of God. The witnesses note that the priests took up the ark. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rose early — to the day's work; the took up the ark — the procession renewed; the diligent in God's work — sparing no pains; the earnest obedience — active to do the will of God.

The witnesses agree Joshua's rising early marks his diligence and zeal in God's service: "Joshua rose early in the morning" to order the second day's procession, sparing no pains to do the LORD's work exactly; the leader's early diligence sets a pattern of earnest, punctual obedience; it teaches that the work of God is to be done with diligence and without delay, the servant rising early to his task, "the king's business required haste" (cf. 1 Sam. 21:8), Joshua active to fulfil the divine command day by day.

So the witnesses make the verse rose early: The witnesses note the rose early: to the day's work. The witnesses note the took up the ark: the procession renewed. The witnesses note the diligent in God's work: sparing no pains. The witnesses note the earnest obedience: active to do the will of God. The expositors observe the rose early: to the day's work. The expositors observe the took up the ark: the procession renewed. The expositors observe the diligent in God's work: sparing no pains. The expositors observe the earnest obedience: active to do the will of God. The believer is taught to mark the rose early, the took up the ark, the diligent in God's work, the earnest obedience, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that Joshua rose early to renew the procession — his diligence and zeal in God's service, the work of God done without delay, the servant earnest to fulfil the divine command.
13:
"And seven priests bearing seven trumpets of rams' horns before the ark of the LORD went on continually, and blew with the trumpets..." The witnesses note the procession of the second day, the trumpets blowing continually before the ark. The witnesses note that Gill refers to the order already given. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the priests going on continually and blowing the trumpets, the armed men before. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seven priests — before the ark; the went on continually — the same order kept; the blew the trumpets — day after day; the patient constancy — not weary in well doing (Gal. 6:9).

The witnesses agree the second day repeated the first exactly, the same solemn order kept: "seven priests... went on continually, and blew with the trumpets," the armed men and rereward in their places; the unwearied repetition, day after day, of the same act of faith shows the patient constancy required, no variation and no haste; it teaches that faith holds steadily to God's appointed way, repeating the duty though the end tarries, the trumpets sounding on "continually," the people persevering in obedience until the promised hour, "let us not be weary in well doing" (Gal. 6:9).

So the witnesses make the verse went on continually: The witnesses note the seven priests: before the ark. The witnesses note the went on continually: the same order kept. The witnesses note the blew the trumpets: day after day. The witnesses note the patient constancy: not weary in well doing (Gal. 6:9). The expositors observe the seven priests: before the ark. The expositors observe the went on continually: the same order kept. The expositors observe the blew the trumpets: day after day. The expositors observe the patient constancy: not weary in well doing (Gal. 6:9). The believer is taught to mark the seven priests, the went on continually, the blew the trumpets, the patient constancy, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that the second day repeated the procession, the trumpets blowing continually — the patient constancy of faith, holding steadily to God's way though the end tarries, not weary in well doing (Gal. 6:9).
14:
"And the second day they compassed the city once, and returned into the camp: so they did six days." The witnesses note the city compassed once on the second day and so for six days together. The witnesses note that Gill notes they went round once only, as on the first. The witnesses note that Clarke doubts the whole host went each day, the fighting men alone being a vast number. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the compassed once — the second day; the returned to camp — no result yet; the so they did six days — the unvaried obedience; the faith awaiting the time — though it tarry, wait for it (Hab. 2:3).

The witnesses agree the same act was faithfully repeated through all six days: "they compassed the city once... so they did six days," the unvaried obedience continued without sign of result; the six days' marching, with nothing yet to show, was the steady walk of faith awaiting God's appointed seventh day; it teaches that the people of God are to persevere in the path of obedience through long delay, the promise sure though the wall stands, faith holding on till the set time, "though it tarry, wait for it; because it will surely come" (Hab. 2:3), the six days' patience preparing the seventh day's triumph.

So the witnesses make the verse six days: The witnesses note the compassed once: the second day. The witnesses note the returned to camp: no result yet. The witnesses note the so they did six days: the unvaried obedience. The witnesses note the faith awaiting the time: though it tarry, wait for it (Hab. 2:3). The expositors observe the compassed once: the second day. The expositors observe the returned to camp: no result yet. The expositors observe the so they did six days: the unvaried obedience. The expositors observe the faith awaiting the time: though it tarry, wait for it (Hab. 2:3). The believer is taught to mark the compassed once, the returned to camp, the so they did six days, the faith awaiting the time, and to trust that the battle is the LORD's. The verse thus records that the city was compassed once each day for six days — the steady walk of faith awaiting God's set time, the promise sure though the wall stood, the six days' patience preparing the triumph (Hab. 2:3).
15:
"And it came to pass on the seventh day, that they rose early about the dawning of the day, and compassed the city after the same manner seven times..." The witnesses note the seventh day, when they rose at dawn and compassed the city seven times. The witnesses note that Gill notes some Jews say it was the sabbath; whether or not, one of the seven days must have been a sabbath, in which the procession was made. The witnesses note that they compassed it seven times that day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the seventh day — the climax of obedience; the rose early — at the dawning; the seven times — the redoubled labour; the the last exertion — reaping if we faint not (Gal. 6:9).

The witnesses agree the seventh day brought the climax of the patient obedience, the sevenfold march: rising "early about the dawning of the day," they compassed the city "seven times," redoubling the labour of the former days; the multiplied circuits of the last day marked the fulness of the test before the promised deliverance; it teaches that faith's greatest exertion often comes just before the victory, the people pressing round seven times in one day, the long patience now crowned, "in due season we shall reap, if we faint not" (Gal. 6:9), the seventh day's sevenfold march at the dawn of triumph.

So the witnesses make the verse seven times: The witnesses note the the seventh day: the climax of obedience. The witnesses note the rose early: at the dawning. The witnesses note the seven times: the redoubled labour. The witnesses note the the last exertion: reaping if we faint not (Gal. 6:9). The expositors observe the the seventh day: the climax of obedience. The expositors observe the rose early: at the dawning. The expositors observe the seven times: the redoubled labour. The expositors observe the the last exertion: reaping if we faint not (Gal. 6:9). The believer is taught to mark the the seventh day, the rose early, the seven times, the the last exertion, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that on the seventh day they rose early and compassed the city seven times — the fulness of the test before deliverance, faith's greatest exertion just before the victory, reaping if we faint not (Gal. 6:9).
16:
"And it came to pass at the seventh time, when the priests blew with the trumpets, Joshua said unto the people, Shout; for the LORD hath given you the city." The witnesses note Joshua, at the seventh circuit, bidding the people shout, for the LORD had given them the city. The witnesses note that Gill notes the seventh time of going round on the seventh day. The witnesses note that Henry notes the people had religiously observed the orders, and now Joshua told them the LORD had given them the city. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the at the seventh time — the appointed moment; the Joshua said, Shout — the summons to faith; the the LORD hath given — the ground of the shout; the triumph before sight — always caused to triumph (2 Cor. 2:14).

The witnesses agree the appointed moment came, and Joshua summoned the shout of faith: at "the seventh time" he bade the people "Shout; for the LORD hath given you the city"; the ground of the shout was the word of promise — not that the wall had fallen, but that God had given it, the victory claimed by faith before it was seen; Henry marks the people's religious obedience now rewarded; it teaches that faith shouts for the victory on the strength of God's word, triumphing before the sight, "thanks be to God, which always causeth us to triumph" (2 Cor. 2:14), the shout raised because the LORD had given the city.

So the witnesses make the verse the shout of faith: The witnesses note the at the seventh time: the appointed moment. The witnesses note the Joshua said, Shout: the summons to faith. The witnesses note the the LORD hath given: the ground of the shout. The witnesses note the triumph before sight: always caused to triumph (2 Cor. 2:14). The expositors observe the at the seventh time: the appointed moment. The expositors observe the Joshua said, Shout: the summons to faith. The expositors observe the the LORD hath given: the ground of the shout. The expositors observe the triumph before sight: always caused to triumph (2 Cor. 2:14). The believer is taught to mark the at the seventh time, the Joshua said, Shout, the the LORD hath given, the triumph before sight, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that Joshua bade the people shout, for the LORD had given the city — the shout of faith claiming the victory on God's word before it was seen, always caused to triumph (2 Cor. 2:14).
17:
"And the city shall be accursed, even it, and all that are therein, to the LORD: only Rahab the harlot shall live, she and all that are with her in the house, because she hid the messengers that we sent." The witnesses note the city devoted to destruction — a cherem to the LORD — only Rahab and her house spared. The witnesses note that Gill notes "accursed" is a "cherem," devoted to the Lord. The witnesses note that Henry notes the people had observed the orders, and now were told the LORD had given the city. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the city accursed — a cherem to the LORD; the all therein — devoted in judgment; the only Rahab shall live — mercy in the midst; the saved by faith — she received the spies (Heb. 11:31).

The witnesses agree the doom of Jericho was a "cherem" — wholly devoted to the LORD in judgment, the firstfruits of the conquest given up to him: "the city shall be accursed... to the LORD"; yet in the midst of the doomed city, mercy spared one — "only Rahab... shall live... because she hid the messengers"; her faith, shown in receiving the spies, saved her and all her house; it teaches that God's judgment on the doomed is exact, yet mercy rescues the believing even from the heart of destruction, Rahab saved by faith, "the harlot perished not with them that believed not, when she had received the spies" (Heb. 11:31).

So the witnesses make the verse the cherem and Rahab: The witnesses note the the city accursed: a cherem to the LORD. The witnesses note the all therein: devoted in judgment. The witnesses note the only Rahab shall live: mercy in the midst. The witnesses note the saved by faith: she received the spies (Heb. 11:31). The expositors observe the the city accursed: a cherem to the LORD. The expositors observe the all therein: devoted in judgment. The expositors observe the only Rahab shall live: mercy in the midst. The expositors observe the saved by faith: she received the spies (Heb. 11:31). The believer is taught to mark the the city accursed, the all therein, the only Rahab shall live, the saved by faith, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that the city was devoted to destruction, only Rahab spared for hiding the spies — judgment exact yet mercy rescuing the believing, Rahab saved by faith from the heart of the doomed city (Heb. 11:31).
18:
"And ye, in any wise keep yourselves from the accursed thing, lest ye make yourselves accursed... and make the camp of Israel a curse, and trouble it." The witnesses note the solemn charge to keep from the accursed thing, lest the taker bring a curse on all the camp. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were to keep from laying hold on, secreting, or enjoying as their own what was devoted to another use. The witnesses note that taking it would make themselves and the camp accursed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the keep yourselves — from the devoted spoil; the the accursed thing — not to be taken; the lest ye be accursed — one sin guilting all; the the camp troubled — achan's coming sin (Josh. 7:1).

The witnesses agree the warning guards the people from a deadly sin — the coveting of the devoted spoil: "keep yourselves from the accursed thing, lest ye... make the camp of Israel a curse"; what was given to God was not to be taken for private gain, and the sin of one in this could bring guilt and trouble on the whole; the warning, soon proved true in Achan, teaches that one man's secret sin may bring a curse on the whole people, and that what is devoted to God must never be turned to selfish use, "there is a sin unto... trouble," the hidden covetousness troubling all the camp (cf. Josh. 7:1).

So the witnesses make the verse the accursed thing: The witnesses note the keep yourselves: from the devoted spoil. The witnesses note the the accursed thing: not to be taken. The witnesses note the lest ye be accursed: one sin guilting all. The witnesses note the the camp troubled: Achan's coming sin (Josh. 7:1). The expositors observe the keep yourselves: from the devoted spoil. The expositors observe the the accursed thing: not to be taken. The expositors observe the lest ye be accursed: one sin guilting all. The expositors observe the the camp troubled: Achan's coming sin (Josh. 7:1). The believer is taught to mark the keep yourselves, the the accursed thing, the lest ye be accursed, the the camp troubled, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that they were to keep from the accursed thing, lest one man's coveting bring a curse on all — what is devoted to God never turned to selfish use, the hidden covetousness troubling the whole camp (Josh. 7:1).
19:
"But all the silver, and gold, and vessels of brass and iron, are consecrated unto the LORD: they shall come into the treasury of the LORD." The witnesses note the precious metals consecrated to the LORD's treasury, not to be taken by any. The witnesses note that Gill notes the silver, gold, and vessels of brass and iron. The witnesses note that Clarke notes they were to come into the treasury of the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the silver and gold — the precious metals; the vessels of brass and iron — the firstfruits of conquest; the consecrated to the LORD — wholly his; the the first and best his — honour him with the firstfruits (Prov. 3:9).

The witnesses agree the precious things of Jericho were reserved wholly for God: "all the silver, and gold, and vessels... are consecrated unto the LORD... into the treasury"; as the firstfruits of the conquest, the wealth of the first city was given up to him, none permitted to enrich himself; the consecration of the spoil teaches that the first and best is God's, and that what is devoted to him is sacred, set apart from common or private use; it teaches reverence for the things of God and the dedication of the firstfruits, the treasure of the doomed city laid up for the LORD, the first of the increase his (cf. Prov. 3:9, honour the LORD with the firstfruits).

So the witnesses make the verse consecrated to the LORD: The witnesses note the silver and gold: the precious metals. The witnesses note the vessels of brass and iron: the firstfruits of conquest. The witnesses note the consecrated to the LORD: wholly his. The witnesses note the the first and best his: honour him with the firstfruits (Prov. 3:9). The expositors observe the silver and gold: the precious metals. The expositors observe the vessels of brass and iron: the firstfruits of conquest. The expositors observe the consecrated to the LORD: wholly his. The expositors observe the the first and best his: honour him with the firstfruits (Prov. 3:9). The believer is taught to mark the silver and gold, the vessels of brass and iron, the consecrated to the LORD, the the first and best his, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that the precious things of Jericho were consecrated to the LORD's treasury — the firstfruits of the conquest wholly his, none enriching himself, the first and best devoted to God (Prov. 3:9).
20:
"So the people shouted when the priests blew with the trumpets... that the wall fell down flat, so that the people went up into the city, every man straight before him, and they took the city." The witnesses note the great shout, the wall falling flat, and the people taking the city. The witnesses note that Gill notes the people shouted as Joshua had charged them. The witnesses note that Clarke notes much learned labour has been spent to prove the shout might naturally cause the fall, but the text ascribes it to God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the people shouted — as charged; the the wall fell flat — by God's power; the not a natural cause — the text ascribes it to God; the the triumph of faith — by faith the walls fell (Heb. 11:30).

The witnesses agree the miracle came at the shout of faith — the wall fell flat by God's power: "the people shouted... the wall fell down flat... and they took the city"; Clarke notes the vain attempts to explain it by natural cause, when the text plainly makes it God's work; the wall fell not by their shout's force but at it, by the hand of God rewarding their faith; it teaches that the strongholds of the enemy fall before the power of God answering the faith of his people, "by faith the walls of Jericho fell down" (Heb. 11:30), the believing shout and the falling wall the triumph of faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the wall fell: The witnesses note the the people shouted: as charged. The witnesses note the the wall fell flat: by God's power. The witnesses note the not a natural cause: the text ascribes it to God. The witnesses note the the triumph of faith: by faith the walls fell (Heb. 11:30). The expositors observe the the people shouted: as charged. The expositors observe the the wall fell flat: by God's power. The expositors observe the not a natural cause: the text ascribes it to God. The expositors observe the the triumph of faith: by faith the walls fell (Heb. 11:30). The believer is taught to mark the the people shouted, the the wall fell flat, the not a natural cause, the the triumph of faith, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that the people shouted and the wall fell flat, and they took the city — the stronghold falling by God's power answering faith, by faith the walls of Jericho fell down (Heb. 11:30).
21:
"And they utterly destroyed all that was in the city, both man and woman, young and old, and ox, and sheep, and ass, with the edge of the sword." The witnesses note the utter destruction of the city — the cherem of God's judgment carried out. The witnesses note that Gill notes all the inhabitants were destroyed by Joshua's direction and God's order, being guilty of capital crimes — idolatry, incest, and the like. The witnesses note that all was destroyed with the edge of the sword. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the utterly destroyed all — the cherem carried out; the man and beast — the whole city; the with the sword — the judgment executed; the iniquity full — the Amorite's sin ripe (Gen. 15:16).

The witnesses agree the destruction of Jericho was the execution of God's righteous judgment on a people ripe in sin: "they utterly destroyed all... with the edge of the sword," the cherem fulfilled; Gill grounds it in the Canaanites' guilt of idolatry and gross sin, long borne with but now full; the severity, terrible to flesh, was the sentence of the Judge of all the earth on the doomed city, Israel his instrument; it teaches that God's long-deferred judgment falls at last on persistent wickedness, the iniquity of the Amorite now full (cf. Gen. 15:16), Jericho the firstfruits of the judgment on Canaan.

So the witnesses make the verse utterly destroyed: The witnesses note the utterly destroyed all: the cherem carried out. The witnesses note the man and beast: the whole city. The witnesses note the with the sword: the judgment executed. The witnesses note the iniquity full: the Amorite's sin ripe (Gen. 15:16). The expositors observe the utterly destroyed all: the cherem carried out. The expositors observe the man and beast: the whole city. The expositors observe the with the sword: the judgment executed. The expositors observe the iniquity full: the Amorite's sin ripe (Gen. 15:16). The believer is taught to mark the utterly destroyed all, the man and beast, the with the sword, the iniquity full, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that they utterly destroyed all in the city — the cherem of God's righteous judgment on a people ripe in sin, the iniquity of the Amorite now full, Jericho the firstfruits of judgment (Gen. 15:16).
22:
"But Joshua had said unto the two men that had spied out the country, Go into the harlot's house, and bring out thence the woman, and all that she hath, as ye sware unto her." The witnesses note Joshua sending the two spies to bring out Rahab and her household, as they had sworn. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua had said this to the spies before the people entered, perhaps before the walls fell. The witnesses note that they were to bring her out as they sware unto her. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the two spies — sent to her house; the bring out the woman — and all she had; the as ye sware — the oath honoured; the faith kept — the LORD faithful (2 Thess. 3:3).

The witnesses agree the saving of Rahab was the faithful keeping of the oath the spies had sworn her: Joshua sent the very men who had pledged their word to "bring out... the woman, and all that she hath, as ye sware"; the honouring of the promise amid the general destruction shows the sacredness of a vow and God's care to keep faith with the believing; it teaches that God remembers his promises to those who trust him, sparing them in the day of judgment, the oath kept and Rahab delivered, "the LORD is faithful, who shall stablish you" (2 Thess. 3:3), the pledged word made good.

So the witnesses make the verse bring out Rahab: The witnesses note the the two spies: sent to her house. The witnesses note the bring out the woman: and all she had. The witnesses note the as ye sware: the oath honoured. The witnesses note the faith kept: the LORD faithful (2 Thess. 3:3). The expositors observe the the two spies: sent to her house. The expositors observe the bring out the woman: and all she had. The expositors observe the as ye sware: the oath honoured. The expositors observe the faith kept: the LORD faithful (2 Thess. 3:3). The believer is taught to mark the the two spies, the bring out the woman, the as ye sware, the faith kept, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that Joshua sent the spies to bring out Rahab as they had sworn — the oath faithfully kept amid the destruction, God remembering his promise to the believing, the pledged word made good (2 Thess. 3:3).
23:
"And the young men that were spies went in, and brought out Rahab, and her father, and her mother, and her brethren, and all that she had... and left them without the camp of Israel." The witnesses note the spies bringing out Rahab and all her family, set safe outside the camp. The witnesses note that Gill notes the spies knew her house again by the scarlet thread hung at the window. The witnesses note that they left them without the camp of Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the brought out Rahab — and all her family; the the scarlet thread — marking the house; the all that she had — saved for her faith; the the token of redemption — when I see the blood (Exod. 12:13).

The witnesses agree the deliverance of Rahab extended to her whole household, known by the scarlet thread: the spies "brought out Rahab, and her father... and all that she had," the token of the scarlet line marking the house to be saved; the saving of all her kin for her faith shows the breadth of the mercy that came through her believing; the scarlet thread, like the blood on the doorposts, has been read as a figure of redemption by the blood of Christ, the marked house spared; it teaches that faith brings salvation to a household, and that the token of redemption distinguishes those who shall be saved, "when I see the blood, I will pass over you" (Exod. 12:13), Rahab's house delivered by the scarlet sign.

So the witnesses make the verse Rahab brought out: The witnesses note the brought out Rahab: and all her family. The witnesses note the the scarlet thread: marking the house. The witnesses note the all that she had: saved for her faith. The witnesses note the the token of redemption: when I see the blood (Exod. 12:13). The expositors observe the brought out Rahab: and all her family. The expositors observe the the scarlet thread: marking the house. The expositors observe the all that she had: saved for her faith. The expositors observe the the token of redemption: when I see the blood (Exod. 12:13). The believer is taught to mark the brought out Rahab, the the scarlet thread, the all that she had, the the token of redemption, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that the spies brought out Rahab and all her household, known by the scarlet thread — faith saving a household, the scarlet sign a figure of redemption, the marked house spared (Exod. 12:13).
24:
"And they burnt the city with fire, and all that was therein: only the silver, and the gold, and the vessels of brass and of iron, they put into the treasury of the house of the LORD." The witnesses note the city burned, only the consecrated metals laid up in the LORD's treasury. The witnesses note that Gill notes the city burnt with fire, as Babylon the great, of which it was an emblem, will be burnt. The witnesses note that the silver and gold were put into the treasury. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the burnt with fire — the doomed city; the all that was therein — wholly destroyed; the the metals to the treasury — what is God's preserved; the an emblem of Babylon — utterly burned with fire (Rev. 18:8).

The witnesses agree the burning of Jericho completed its devotion to destruction, the metals alone reserved for God: "they burnt the city with fire... only the silver... and gold... they put into the treasury"; Gill sees in doomed Jericho an emblem of "Babylon the great," likewise to be burned; the total destruction, with only the consecrated wealth preserved, marks the thoroughness of God's judgment and the sanctity of what is his; it teaches that the city of sin is given to the fire while what is consecrated to God is kept, a figure of the final overthrow of the world that opposes him, "Babylon... shall be utterly burned with fire" (Rev. 18:8).

So the witnesses make the verse the city burned: The witnesses note the burnt with fire: the doomed city. The witnesses note the all that was therein: wholly destroyed. The witnesses note the the metals to the treasury: what is God's preserved. The witnesses note the an emblem of Babylon: utterly burned with fire (Rev. 18:8). The expositors observe the burnt with fire: the doomed city. The expositors observe the all that was therein: wholly destroyed. The expositors observe the the metals to the treasury: what is God's preserved. The expositors observe the an emblem of Babylon: utterly burned with fire (Rev. 18:8). The believer is taught to mark the burnt with fire, the all that was therein, the the metals to the treasury, the an emblem of Babylon, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that they burned the city, only the consecrated metals laid up — the thoroughness of God's judgment, doomed Jericho an emblem of Babylon, the city of sin given to the fire (Rev. 18:8).
25:
"And Joshua saved Rahab the harlot alive, and her father's household, and all that she had; and she dwelleth in Israel even unto this day; because she hid the messengers..." The witnesses note Rahab saved alive and dwelling in Israel, for hiding the messengers. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua saved her from the sword; Josephus says he gave her fields and held her in great honour and esteem. The witnesses note that she dwelt in Israel for hiding the messengers. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the saved Rahab alive — from the sword; the dwelleth in Israel — received among God's people; the for hiding the messengers — her faith rewarded; the a trophy of grace — an ancestress of Christ (Matt. 1:5).

The witnesses agree the saving of Rahab ended in her full reception into Israel — a Canaanite harlot, by faith, made a daughter of the covenant: "Joshua saved Rahab... and she dwelleth in Israel"; she who had hidden the spies in faith was not only spared but honoured, given a home and place among God's people; the gospel marvel is greater still, for Rahab the harlot became an ancestress of David and of Christ (Matt. 1:5); it teaches that faith brings the outcast and the sinner into the people of God, justified by works that proved her faith (James 2:25), Rahab the trophy of grace dwelling among the redeemed.

So the witnesses make the verse Rahab saved: The witnesses note the saved Rahab alive: from the sword. The witnesses note the dwelleth in Israel: received among God's people. The witnesses note the for hiding the messengers: her faith rewarded. The witnesses note the a trophy of grace: an ancestress of Christ (Matt. 1:5). The expositors observe the saved Rahab alive: from the sword. The expositors observe the dwelleth in Israel: received among God's people. The expositors observe the for hiding the messengers: her faith rewarded. The expositors observe the a trophy of grace: an ancestress of Christ (Matt. 1:5). The believer is taught to mark the saved Rahab alive, the dwelleth in Israel, the for hiding the messengers, the a trophy of grace, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that Joshua saved Rahab, and she dwelt in Israel — the Canaanite harlot by faith made a daughter of the covenant, ancestress of Christ, the trophy of grace dwelling among the redeemed (Matt. 1:5; James 2:25).
26:
"And Joshua adjured them at that time, saying, Cursed be the man before the LORD, that riseth up and buildeth this city Jericho..." The witnesses note Joshua's solemn curse on whoever should rebuild Jericho. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua adjured them when the city was burnt and spoiled, not man by man but in a general way. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the curse on the man who should build Jericho. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Joshua adjured them — with a solemn curse; the cursed be the man — that rebuilds it; the a perpetual monument — of God's judgment; the the curse fulfilled — in Hiel the Bethelite (1 Kings 16:34).

The witnesses agree Joshua sealed the destruction with a binding curse on any who should rebuild the doomed city: "cursed be the man... that riseth up and buildeth this city Jericho"; the city given to God's judgment was to lie a perpetual monument of his wrath, not raised again; the curse was fulfilled in after times when Hiel the Bethelite rebuilt it and lost his sons (1 Kings 16:34); it teaches that what God has devoted to judgment is not to be restored in defiance of his word, and that his curses are as sure as his blessings, the word of Joshua standing centuries till it was made good.

So the witnesses make the verse the curse on Jericho: The witnesses note the Joshua adjured them: with a solemn curse. The witnesses note the cursed be the man: that rebuilds it. The witnesses note the a perpetual monument: of God's judgment. The witnesses note the the curse fulfilled: in Hiel the Bethelite (1 Kings 16:34). The expositors observe the Joshua adjured them: with a solemn curse. The expositors observe the cursed be the man: that rebuilds it. The expositors observe the a perpetual monument: of God's judgment. The expositors observe the the curse fulfilled: in Hiel the Bethelite (1 Kings 16:34). The believer is taught to mark the Joshua adjured them, the cursed be the man, the a perpetual monument, the the curse fulfilled, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that Joshua cursed whoever should rebuild Jericho — the doomed city to lie a monument of judgment, the curse sure and fulfilled at last in Hiel (1 Kings 16:34).
27:
"So the LORD was with Joshua; and his fame was noised throughout all the country." The witnesses note the LORD with Joshua, his fame spread through all the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Lord counselled, directed, prospered, and succeeded him; the Targum, "the Word of the Lord was for the help of Joshua" — the essential Word, Christ, the Captain of the host. The witnesses note that his fame was noised throughout the country. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD was with Joshua — directing and prospering; the the Word of the Lord — christ the Captain (Gill); the his fame spread — through all the land; the success from his presence — i will be with thee (Josh. 1:5).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes by ascribing all to the LORD's presence with his servant: "the LORD was with Joshua," directing and prospering him, and so "his fame was noised throughout all the country"; Gill notes the Targum reads "the Word of the Lord," that is, Christ the Captain of the host, with him; the victory and renown of Joshua flowed from God's being with him; it teaches that the success of God's servants is wholly from his presence, the same promise that began the book now proved, "I will be with thee" (Josh. 1:5), the LORD with Joshua the secret of his triumph.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD with Joshua: The witnesses note the the LORD was with Joshua: directing and prospering. The witnesses note the the Word of the Lord: Christ the Captain (Gill). The witnesses note the his fame spread: through all the land. The witnesses note the success from his presence: I will be with thee (Josh. 1:5). The expositors observe the the LORD was with Joshua: directing and prospering. The expositors observe the the Word of the Lord: Christ the Captain (Gill). The expositors observe the his fame spread: through all the land. The expositors observe the success from his presence: I will be with thee (Josh. 1:5). The believer is taught to mark the the LORD was with Joshua, the the Word of the Lord, the his fame spread, the success from his presence, and to trust the God who throws down strongholds. The verse thus records that the LORD was with Joshua and his fame spread — the victory flowing wholly from God's presence with his servant, the promise proved, I will be with thee (Josh. 1:5).
